preamble: The shift from the voiced bilabial stop /b/ to the palatal glide /j/ is an instance of significant lenition and palatalization. This change often happens in the vicinity of high front vowels, where the place of articulation for /b/ moves towards the palate, becomes a fricative or approximant, and eventually merges with /j/.

examples.note: In some fast speech contexts, the lenition of /b/ between vowels can lead to a glide, especially before /i/.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English.
